mcqs.question: Which memory segment is used for static memory allocation in C++?
mcqs.explanation: Static memory allocation occurs at compile time and uses the stack memory segment. Variables are automatically allocated and deallocated based on scope.

mcqs.question: What operator is used to dynamically allocate memory in C++?
mcqs.explanation: The 'new' operator is the C++ way to allocate dynamic memory. It not only allocates memory but also calls constructors for objects.

mcqs.question: What will happen if 'delete' is called twice on the same pointer?

options.text: Memory is freed twice causing undefined behavior

mcqs.explanation: Calling delete twice on the same pointer causes undefined behavior (double-free). The pointer becomes dangling and accessing it is dangerous.

mcqs.question: Which of the following correctly allocates an array of 10 integers dynamically?
mcqs.explanation: 'new int[10]' allocates array of 10 integers. malloc can also be used but is C-style. 'new int(10)' allocates single integer with value 10.

mcqs.question: What is a dangling pointer?

options.text: A pointer that has not been initialized
A pointer to memory that has been freed
A pointer that points to NULL
A pointer to a local variable

mcqs.explanation: A dangling pointer references memory that has been deallocated. Using it leads to undefined behavior. Always set pointers to nullptr after deletion.

mcqs.question: What is the output of: int* p = new int; cout << p;
mcqs.explanation: A pointer stores a memory address. Printing the pointer displays the hexadecimal address of the allocated heap memory, not the value stored.

mcqs.question: Which smart pointer automatically deletes memory when it goes out of scope?
mcqs.explanation: unique_ptr is an exclusive ownership smart pointer that automatically deletes the managed resource when it goes out of scope.

mcqs.question: What is the main disadvantage of dynamic memory allocation?

options.text: Risk of memory leaks and dangling pointers

mcqs.explanation: Dynamic allocation requires manual memory management. Forgetting to free memory causes leaks; accessing freed memory causes undefined behavior.

mcqs.question: What does 'new int[5]' return if memory allocation fails?
mcqs.explanation: In C++, 'new' throws std::bad_alloc exception on failure (not-nhrowing version available with std::nothrow).

mcqs.question: Which is NOT a part of the Rule of Three in C++?
mcqs.explanation: Rule of Three requires: destructor, copy constructor, and copy assignment operator. Move constructor is part of Rule of Five in modern C++.

mcqs.question: What is the correct way to deallocate a dynamically allocated array?
mcqs.explanation: Arrays allocated with new[] must be freed with delete[]. Using delete instead causes undefined behavior.

mcqs.question: What is memory fragmentation?

options.text: Scattered free memory blocks making allocation difficult

mcqs.explanation: Memory fragmentation occurs when free memory is broken into small non-contiguous blocks, making it impossible to allocate large blocks even when total free memory is sufficient.

mcqs.question: What is the difference between stack and heap?

options.text: No difference, they are the same
Stack is faster but limited; Heap is slower but larger
Heap is faster but limited; Stack is slower but larger
Stack is for programs, Heap is for data

mcqs.explanation: Stack has fast access but limited size; Heap is larger but slower. Stack allocation/deallocation is automatic; Heap requires manual management.

mcqs.question: What happens if 'new' is used but 'delete' is never called?

options.text: Nothing happens, memory is freed automatically

mcqs.explanation: Not calling delete on dynamically allocated memory causes a memory leak. The memory remains allocated until program terminates.

mcqs.question: What is the default value of uninitialized pointer in C++?
mcqs.explanation: Uninitialized pointers contain garbage values (undefined). Always initialize pointers to nullptr (C++11) or NULL before use.

mcqs.question: Which header provides smart pointers in C++?
mcqs.explanation: Smart pointers are defined in the <memory> header file in C++. This is part of the Standard Template Library (STL).

mcqs.question: What is the time complexity of allocating memory with 'new'?

options.text: O(n) where n is heap size
Depends on allocator and memory state

mcqs.explanation: Time complexity varies based on memory allocator implementation and current heap state. In practice, it's often amortized O(1) but can be higher.
After delete, the memory is freed but the pointer still holds the old address. Dereferencing it gives undefined behavior (often garbage value).

mcqs.question: Which type of allocation is preferred for unknown array size at compile time?
mcqs.explanation: Dynamic allocation on heap is used when array size is unknown at compile time. The size can be determined at runtime and memory allocated accordingly.

mcqs.question: What is placement new used for?
mcqs.explanation: Placement new constructs an object at a specified memory address. It doesn't allocate memory, just calls constructor at given location.
